coredata.dc:title: Structural transition of gold octahedrons to nanoprisms induced by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Sejung
Park, Taehyun
Oh, Geun-Hyeong
Kim, Bo-Seok
Ju, Yu Seung
Rho, Won-Yeop
coredata.dc:description: Colloidal novel metallic nanoparticles have drawn much attention due to their localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects. It is important to produced homogeneously morphological features in sizes and shapes to quantify the analytes of interest through LSPR properties. Here, we demonstrated a simple synthetic strategies to produce Au nanoprisms by adding nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as a co-capping agent. The NCQDs were prepared via graphitization by selecting citric acid as a carbon source and either urea or polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a nitrogen source. In the presence of NCQDs, the formation of Au nanoprisms can be achieved with approximately 2 μm of edge length when the PEI-derivative NCQDs. The mechanism of Au nanoprism formation were investigated by electron microscopy techniques to investigate the effect of NCQDs on the formation of Au crystals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Au nanocrystals
Morphological transition
Co-capping agent
Hydrothermal synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Au octahedrons and nanoprisms
Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
Morphological transition of Au nanocrystals with NCQDs
Mechanism of morphological transition Au nanocrystals

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the growth of Au nanocrystals: (a) in the absence of NCQDs, (b) in the presence of NCQDs.
The Au octahedron nanocrystals. (a) SEM image of individual and (b) self-assembled structures of Au octahedrons. (c) TEM image of Au octahedrons, and (d) their lattice structure.

Characterization of NCQDs. TEM images of (a) UNCQDs, (b) PNCQDs (inset: the lattice structure of PNCQDs with 0.22 nm). The D- and G-band of Raman spectrum of (c) UNCQDs and (d) PNCQDs. UV–vis spectrum of (e) UNCQDs and (f) PNCQDs.

Formation of Au nanocrystals using CTAB. (a) SEM, (b) TEM images of Au nanoprism in the presence of UNCQDs, and (c) SEM image of separated Au nanoprisms by capillary forces. (d) SEM, (e) TEM images of Au prism by adopting PNCQDs, and (f) SEM image of self-assembled Au prisms by capillary forces.

Characterization of Au nanocrystals. UV–vis spectrum of (a) Au octahedrons, (b) Au triangles produced in the presence of UNCQDs, and (c) Au triangles produced in the presence of PNCQDs. XRD patterns of (d) Au octahedrons, (e) Au triangles synthesized in the presence of UNCQDs, and (f) Au triangles synthesized in the presence of PNCQDs.
Formation of Au nanocrystals using CTAC. (a, b) SEM images of Au nanoprism with wrinkled surfaces in the presence of UNCQDs, and (c,d) SEM images of Au nanoprisms by using PNCQDs.

head.ce:title: Structural transition of gold octahedrons to nanoprisms induced by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of International Engineering and Science, Jeonbuk National University, 567 Baekjedae-ro, Jeonju-si, Jeollabuk-do, 54896, South Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Graduate School of Integrated Energy-AI, Jeonbuk National University, 567 Baekjedae-ro, Jeonju-si, Jeollabuk-do 54896, South Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Colloidal novel metallic nanoparticles have drawn much attention due to their localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects. It is important to produced homogeneously morphological features in sizes and shapes to quantify the analytes of interest through LSPR properties. Here, we demonstrated a simple synthetic strategies to produce Au nanoprisms by adding nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as a co-capping agent. The NCQDs were prepared via graphitization by selecting citric acid as a carbon source and either urea or polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a nitrogen source. In the presence of NCQDs, the formation of Au nanoprisms can be achieved with approximately 2 μm of edge length when the PEI-derivative NCQDs. The mechanism of Au nanoprism formation were investigated by electron microscopy techniques to investigate the effect of NCQDs on the formation of Au crystals.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Exquisite control of morphological architectures have been desired for the high-performance of applications in the field of the biosensing, biomedicine, and catalysis [1–5]. In particular, the physicochemical properties of metallic nanocrystals are highly dependent on their size, shape, crystal structure, and exposed surfaces [6–10]. Therefore, the strategy of fine control of morphological homogeneity is required for quantitative analysis during the nanocrystal growth, which can be achieved by selecting molecular capping agents to induce the direct nanocrystal growth in a selective face of nanocrystals [6,7,10–12].

For the metallic nanocrystal formation, wet chemical process has been well established to produce the finely controlled nanostructures in desired sizes and shapes, which enable them to enhance the LSPR properties as well as the sensitivity to analytes of interest. In general, the seed-mediated process are widely adopted for the formation of decent and controllable morphologies of gold nanocrystals or bimetallic nanocrystals [12–14]. Otherwise, the metallic nanocrystals were grown on supporting materials such as graphitic carbon nanostructures (e.g. carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, and carbon quantum dots) [15–18]. For example, the formation of Au nanocomposites on graphene oxide takes advantage of the intrinsic sp
2 - and sp
3 ‑carbon lattice; the oxygen-included functional groups position Au ions on the basal plane of graphene oxide and the Au nanocrystals are grown along the carbon lattice, producing the various morphologies of Au nanocrystals [17]. Among the Au nanocrystals, Au nanoprisms have been much drawn attention due to their LSPR performances in the visible light region, resulting from their high anisotropy [19–21]. The seeded-mediated growth process is the most efficient way for producing the controllable and homogeneous morphologies of Au nanoprisms [22–24]. However, the formation of Au nanoprisms with a crystal structure of face-centered cubic (f.c.c.) is not thermodynamically favorable, which results in impeding high yield of Au nanoprism for practical uses [11]. To achieve the formation of Au nanoprisms with anisotropic nanostructures, it is critical to adjust the ratio of capping agents and reducing metal ions (e.g. seed particles or the nuclei). In addition, the use of a co-capping agent played an important role in producing well-defined nanostructures with high uniformity [6,7,9,10,30]. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) composed of sp
2 - and sp
3 ‑carbon bonding have a high affinity to Au ions due to the presence of nitrogen-included functional groups, which can lead to an accelerated deposition of Au nuclei into the preferential sites. In addition, NCQDs can functionalize the surface of semiconducting nanomaterials, which enhances photovoltaic and optoelectronic device performance and operational stability as assessed by energy band alignment [25–29].

With this regards, here, we developed a simple hydrothermal process to produce Au nanocrystals by employing either hexacetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) or chloride (CTAC) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as a co-capping agents. At the fixed molar ratio of [CTAX]/[HAuCl4] = 60, the versatile capabilities of producing Au nanocrystals were examined; the morphological transition of Au nanocrystals from octahedrons to nanoprisms were achieved in the presence of NCQDs. In addition, the densities of amine groups on the surface of NCQDs were largely affect to the resulting morphologies, wherein the edge lengths of Au nanoprisms were approximately 2 μm. Moreover, the surface of Au nanoprism have the wrinkled nanostructures in the replacement of CTAB with CTAC.

Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate trihydrate (HAuCl4 ·3H2O, Aldrich, 99.9%), trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O, 99.9%), hexacetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 95%), and hexacetyltrimethylammonium choloride (CTAC, 98%). citric acid (C6H5O7, 99%), urea (CN2OH4, 99.5%), poly(ethyleneimine) (PEI, Aldrich, Mn ∼ 1.8 K) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co. All the chemicals were used without further purifications.

Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were produced by graphitization of proper precursors such as citric acid, urea, and polyethyleneimine (1.8 K). For all NCQDs, the graphitization of NCQDs were processed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 200 °C for 12 h and citric acid was used for a carbon source. The terminology of NCQDs were determined by choosing a type of nitrogen sources of either urea (UNCQDs) or PEI (PNCQDs). Finally, NCQDs were purified by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 30 min to remove the solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Au octahedrons and nanoprisms
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of Au octahedrons, it can be found elsewhere [25]. In brief, 9.7 mL of DI water (18.2 MΩ), 0.055 g of CTAB, 250 μL of 0.01 M HAuCl4, and 50 μL of 0.1 M trisodium citrate were added to a glass vial with a Teflon-lined polypropylene cap. The CTAB in the final solution is 1.51 × 10−2 M and the molar ratio of [CTAB]/[HAuCl4] = 60. To prevent the temperature gradient during the reaction, the glass vial as a reactor were wrapped with aluminum foil. The sample vials were put into the oven after the reaction temperature at 110 °C were reached. After the reaction, the temperature was naturally cooled down to room temperature, then the products were washed with centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 20 min and redispersed in DI water, repeated three times. Finally, the resulting Au nanocrystals were stored in DI water. For the synthesis of Au nanoprism, the co-capping agent of 0.1 M trisodium citrate were exchanged with NCQDs. 50 μL of NCQDs with a concentration of 5 mg mL−1 were added into the reaction vessel with maintaining the same total volume of 10 mL. The crystallization reaction were proceeded at 110 °C and purification steps were the same with that of Au octahedrons.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was carried out by using FEI Helios Nanolab 660 DualBeam at an acceleration voltage of 2 kV. The morphological features and lattice structures were characterized by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) operating at 200 kV with FEI Tecnai F-20 TEM/STEM. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected by using a Rigaku MAX-2000 diffractometer with Cu radiation installed at the Future Energy Convergence Core Center (FECC). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured with an NEXSA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer system. Raman spectra were measured with a Nanophoton Raman spectrometer installed in the Center for University-wide Research Facilities (CURF) at Jeonbuk National University at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum was recorded with a Jasco V-670 UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer.

A two-step synthetic process were developed to first produce nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs). In the following reaction, the obtained NCQDs were added into the Au nanocrystal formation reaction to investigate the morphological transition of Au nanocrystals. In this reaction, the formation of Au nanocrystals was proceeded either in the absence or presence of NCQDs (Fig. 1a and b).

Before the effect of the presence of NCQDs on the morphological variations were investigated, our initial experiments were established to produce Au octahedrons by maintaining at [CTAX]/[HAuCl4] = 60 (X = Br, Cl) through hydrothermal process at 110 °C for 12 h. For the formation of Au nanocrystals, the capping agents were first chosen to hexacetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and trisodium citrate. The well-defined Au octahedrons were obtained by employing CTAB with homogenous size approximately 35 nm of the edge length. Their individual and self-assembled structures on a Si substrate during the drying process were shown in Fig. 2a and b.

A representative transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of Au octahedron confirmed that their edge length with ∼35 nm in homogeneous sizes, wherein the d-spacing of crystals is 2.35 Å, indicating that octahedrons are bounded by (111) faces, as shown in Fig. 2c–d. The capping agents with CTAB and trisodium citrate leads to the formation of homogenous Au octahedrons with (111) directions, where the efficient incorporation protocols at the molar ratio of [CTAB]/[HAuCl4] is confirmed [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: As demonstrated in Fig. 1, the effect of two different types of nitrogen sources of NCQDs on morphological features were investigated during Au crystallization process. The NCQDs were prepared by using citric acid as a carbon source and either urea or polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw ∼ 1.8 K) as a nitrogen source. Based on the type of nitrogen sources used for the preparation of NCQDs, we refer to UNCQDs (urea) and PNCQDs (PEI), respectively.

In Fig. 3a and b, the representative transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of NCQDs were shown that they are less than 5 nm in diameter, where the both of UNCQDs and PNCQDs seem to have no differences in morphological features. In addition, their lattice distance of NCQDs were 0.22 nm, which are identical to a lattice distance of graphitic nanostructures (Fig. 3b inset) [28,29]. For the graphitic structures of UNCQDs and PNCQDs, they all have the D- and G-bands, corresponding to the sp
3 - and sp
2 ‑carbon bonded structures. Moreover, the relative intensity ratio of IG/ID is characteristic of the effective conjugation length of NCQDs. The D- and G- bands of two types of NCQDs are shown in Fig. 3c and d; the D-bands are 1337 and 1328 cm−1, while the G-bands are 1587 and 1578 cm−1 for UNCQDs and PNCQDs, respectively. For the comparison of the effective conjugation length of NCQDs, the relative ratio of IG/ID is 0.97 and 0.87 for UNCQDs and PNCQDs, respectively. This increase in the relative ratio of IG/ID means that UNCQDs possessed higher effective conjugation lengths than that of PNCQDs, which implied that PNCQDs might have less graphitization degrees and sp
3 ‑carbon bond-based functional groups on the surfaces of PNCQDs [30,31]. The absorbance peaks were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer, with peaks for UNCQDs were observed at 335 nm and peaks for PNCQDs were observed at 360 nm (Fig. 3e and f). We attribute these peaks to the π- π* transition in the aromatic rings (e.g. CC and CN bonds) [25,27]. Additionally, X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) analysis showed that NCQDs consist of sp
2 ‑carbon bonds in the core and sp
3 ‑carbon bonds, including carboxyl and amine groups, on the surface. The deconvolution of N 1 s suggests that nitrogen was chemically bonded into pyridinic N (399.5 eV), pyrrolic N (400.4 eV), and graphitic N (401.0 eV) (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological transition of Au nanocrystals with NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the absence of NCQDs, the capping agents of CTAB and trisodium citrate at the molar ratio of [CTAB]/[HAuCl4] = 60 led to the formation of homogenous Au octahedrons (Fig. 1). The trisodium citrate as a co-capping agent in the Au octahedron formations was exchanged with NCQDs with a concentration of 5 mg mL−1. The molar ratio of [CTAX]/[HAuCl4] (X = Br, Cl) was maintained at 60 and the hydrothermal reaction was proceeded at the same temperature and reaction time (110 °C and 12 h). Due to the production of NCQDs in DMF, 50 μL of only DMF was added into the reaction mixture to exclude any possible effect from a solvent acting as a co-capping agent, or from a co-solvent, on the morphologies of Au nanocrystals. The resulting Au nanocrystals were not meaningfully changed (Fig. S2).

In Fig. 4 , the UNCQDs were adopted as a co-capping agent with CTAB, resulting in multiple shapes of Au nanocrystals, such as octahedrons, truncated nanoprisms, and multi-twinned structures with less than 500 nm of the edge lengths. In addition, those were consisted of Au nanocrystals smaller than 200 nm (Fig. 4b). In the following process, those Au nanocrystals can be separated by capillary forces, where the larger and flat structured-particles resides at the edges and smaller particles moves into the inside. The smaller particles are largely composed of multi-twinned crystals and the large particles (e.g. Au nanoprisms) were assembled at the edges (Fig. S3). A representative SEM image of the self-assembled of Au nanoprisms with the edge length of 150 nm at the edges were shown in Fig. 4c.

Instead of UNCQDs, the introduction of PNCQDs seems to facilitate the growth rate and the formation of Au nanocrystals, resulting in mainly Au prisms with large than 1 μm of edge length, as shown in Fig. 4d. In Fig. 4e, the representative TEM image presented that Au prisms with larger than 2 μm of edge length were produced, as well as the multiple shapes of Au crystals with less than 200 nm were obtained. Likewise, those Au prisms were separated and self-assembled at the edges on a substrate during drying process through capillary forces. As shown in Fig. 4f, those particles can be separated by sizes through capillary actions on the Au nanocrystals, resulting in assembling Au prisms with a micrometer with their triangular faces each other. Likewise, in the case of using PNCQDs, the Au nanocrystals were consisted of various sizes of particles: multi-twinned particles are mostly less than 300 nm and truncated and triangular Au prisms are mostly larger than 1 μm. The optical properties of Au nanocrystals, including Au octahedrons and nanoprisms. Maximum absorption peaks were detected at 520 nm for the Au octahedrons, which is identical to what has been detected in prior literature (Fig. 5a) [30]. Au nanoprisms with an edge length above 2 μm produced using PNCQDs have maximum absorption peaks at 620 nm (Fig. 5c). Meanwhile, the Au nanoprism synthesized using UNCQDs had two maximum peaks at 580 nm and 615 nm (Fig. 5b). As we analyzed the morphological features, we found that when UNCQDs was employed as a co-capping agent during Au nanocrystal formation, Au nanocrystals of a variety of shapes were produced, such as octahedrons, truncated nanoprisms, and multi-twinned structures with less than 500 nm (Fig. 4a-c).

These multiple shapes might result in bimodal absorption peaks due to the principles of collective oscillation of the surface plasmon; the spectral features of Au nanocrystals using UNCQDs would have an overlapped absorption spectrum in-between 520 nm and 620 nm. To confirm the crystallographic information on Au nanocrystals, we examined the XRD pattern of the Au octahedron. We noted that the Au nanoprisms for both the UNCQDs and PNCQDs demonstrated a stronger intensity at the (111) reflection peak that at the peaks at (200), (220), and (311), which is consistent with the formation of a face-centered cubic (fcc) crystal structure (Fig. 5d-f) [31,32].

The capping agent was exchanged into hexacetyltrimethylammonium chloride (CTAC) with the same protocols instead of CTAB; [CTAC]/[HAuCl4] = 60 and hydrothermal process at 110 °C for 12 h. For the comparison, Au nanocrystals were produced by adding 50 μL of only DMF. Those Au nanocrystals resulted in high-index crystals such as octahedrons, tetrahexahedrons, and truncated tetrahedrons (Fig. S4). In the case of using UNCQDs, the resulting nanoplates were rounded-triangles, hexagons, and circular shapes with the nano-sized ridges of wrinkled surfaces.

On the other hand, the myriad shapes of crystal were produced in the presence of PNCQDs, such as octahedrons, tetrahexahedrons, triangles, and hexagonal shapes ( Fig. 6
c and d ). Therefore, it is worth nothing that the usage of NCQDs can take advantage of producing plate-like Au nanocrystals with wrinkled surfaces, while the PNCQDs were not able to find the preferential crystal growth over controlling Au nanocrystals in the hydrothermal process with the molar ratio of [CTAX]/[HAuCl4] = 60 (X = Br, Cl).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of morphological transition Au nanocrystals
ce:section.ce:para: For the formation of a nanocrystal, capping agents play an important role to alter the specific surface free energy by attached the specific types of facets. According to the Wulff theorem, the facets of nanocrystals were stabilized by the capping agent, leading to lower specific surface energy, resulting in the relative growth rate of that type of facets along with the different crystallographic plane. Since the chemisorbed capping agents on a binding site prevents the deposition of additional atoms onto the sites, the shape evolution of nanocrystals is determined with particular facets. In other words, truncated structures is resulting from the slower growth rate of a facet covered by capping agents. As an example, the effect of anions on the nanocrystal structures in the presence of two complementary capping agents, composed of CTAB/trisodium citrates and CTAC/trisodium citrates mixture, as shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. 6. The difference of two capping agent mixtures was in the presence of Br− or Cl− to be deposited on the Au (111) direction. The shapes were evolved into Au octahedrons in the presence of CTAB and high-indexed facets Au nanocrystals in the presence of CTAC. The effect of halide elements on the nanocrystal structures were investigated, resulting in the triangular nanoplate with highly homogeneous sizes [23]. In our case, we found that NCQDs as a co-capping agent induce the formation of triangular Au nanoplates, resulting from preferential deposition of Au nuclei into (111) faces (Fig. 4), indicating NCQDs played an important role in the formation and growth of triangular nanoplates.

In the presence of two complementary capping agents, the trisodium citrates were exchanged into UNCQDs or PNCQDs, since the preferential sites a capping agent to a specific metal surface is determined by functional groups of a capping agent and an intrinsic properties of nanocrystals such as electronic and surface structures. For the Au nanocrystal formed in the presence of the NCQDs, we speculate that the pyridinyl nitrogen in the NCQDs has an effect on the formation of triangular shapes by accelerating the deposition into the (111) faces [37]. The graphitic carbon bondings and the functional groups (e.g. –OH, -COOH and –NH2) may accelerate the growth of preferential facets, resulting in multiple shapes of Au nanocrystals on graphene oxide sheets [15–18]. To investigate the effect of bonding type of nitrogen, we prepared various types of NCQDs using a diverse array of nitrogen-including precursors such as urea and polyethyleneimine (PEI). Both UNCQDs and PNCQDs showed preferential growth of Au nanoprisms from octahedrons (CTAB). A larger amount of pyridinyl nitrogen may accelerate the formation of triangular shapes by depositing Au nuclei into the (111) faces as shown in Fig. 4. Therefore, we concluded that the addition of nitrogen bonding types enhances the formation of triangular nanoplates with entirely (111) planes with thermodynamically stable structures.

In conclusion, we have successfully developed a synthetic approach to produce morphological transitions of Au nanocrystals in the presence of NCQDs via hydrothermal process. At the molar ratio of [CTAB]/[HAuCl4] = 60, the Au octahedrons were produced with 35 nm of edge lengths. By introduction of NCQDs into the Au crystallization instead of trisodium citrate, the Au nanocrystals were produced in the forms of nanoprisms and multi-twinned nanocrystals. Moreover, the surface of Au nanoplates have highly wrinkled surfaces by using CATC instead of CTAB as a stabilizer. In the formation of Au nanocrystal, we can compare the nitrogen-included functional groups are critical factors to induce the lateral growth of Au nanocrystals in the crystal structures of {111} due to the presence of pyridinyl nitrogen in the NCQDs. The presence of pyridinyl nitrogen with high density in the PNCQDs induced the higher growth rate up to a few microns in the edge lengths. In cases where the sensing technologies will be limited by the available concentrations of analytes, this synthetic route might provide sufficient and high enough to provide signals of anlalytes by taking advantages of those Au nanocrystals as a surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrates.

S. K. designed and performed the research, including synthesis and HRTEM. T. P performed synthesis, UV–vis measurement, and XRD analysis. G. -H. O. did the XRD analysis and data curation. B. -S. K. did the Raman measurement and data curation. Y. S. J. did the UV–vis measurement. W. -Y. R. designed and supervised the research, and provided funding resources. All authors prepared the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that influenced or could appear to have influenced the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by research funds for newly appointed professors of Jeonbuk National University in 2020 and by “Human Resources Program in Energy Technology” of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP), which received financial from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy, Republic of Korea (No. 20204010600470).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colcom.2022.100675.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Subnanomolar sensitivity of filter paper-based SERS sensor for pesticide detection by hydrophobicity change of paper surface

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal syntheses of gold nanocrystals: from icosahedral to its truncated form
